coredata.dc:title: Solvent-mediated in situ growth and assembly of gold nanoparticles@carbon dots for rapid colorimetric nonenzymatic alcohol sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Chaoying
Tong, Xia
Cao, Yuanxin
Cai, Guihan
Wang, Tongtao
Wei, Qisheng
Shi, Shuyun
Guo, Ying
coredata.dc:description: The fundamental mechanism underlying solvent-mediated carbon dots (CDs) reduction of HAuCl4 to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and the surface characteristics deciding their dispersion and aggregation, are largely unclear. Monodispersed core-shell AuNPs@CDs(water) (21.9 ± 2.7 nm) were in situ synthesized in water, while aggregated AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (102.6 ± 22.0 nm) were found in ethanol. We propose that the transition is mediated by solvent through changing the solubility and reducing performance of CDs, and interactions among oxidized CDs, AuNPs and solvent. Water with higher polarity promotes CDs to rapidly reduce Au3+ into Au0, and forms a stronger electrical double layer for slow growth of nearly neutral AuNPs. Concurrently, oxidized CDs(water) with higher contents of carboxyl groups coordinate with AuNPs, and stabilize AuNPs by strong electrostatic repulsive forces (zeta potential at −23.5 mV). Red color was obtained in water system. However, ethanol with lower polarity deduces the aggregation of AuNPs. Moreover, oxidized CDs(ethanol) with lower contents of carboxyl groups coordinate with AuNPs, and interact with each other through strong hydrogen bonds to form carboxyl dimers, which further deduces aggregation. Then blue color was achieved in ethanol system. Therefore, a facile AuNPs@CDs-based colorimetric ethanol sensor (linear range, 20.0%–90.0%, v/v, R 2 = 0.999) with high selectivity was proposed by measuring the plasmon resonance absorbance change of AuNPs. Real sample analysis of liquors based on the standard addition method showed satisfying accuracy and excellent reproducibility. The proposed method provides new insight into nonenzymatic ethanol detection, and valuable information for the development of AuNPs-based colorimetric sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Gold nanoparticles
Growth
Aggregation
Ethanol

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Separation of oxidized CDs from AuNPs@CDs
Mechanism of solvent mediating the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis spectra of CDs in water (a), AuNPs@CDs(water) (b), and separated oxidized CDs(water) (c) in water system; (B) UV–vis spectra of CDs in ethanol (a), AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (b), and separated oxidized CDs(ethanol) (c) in ethanol system. Insets are the corresponding photos. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FTIR spectra of CDs (a), separated oxidized CDs(water) (b) and oxidized CDs(ethanol) (c), (B) XRD spectra of AuNPs@CDs(water) (a) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of CDs (A), AuNPs@CDs(water) (C) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (E); particle size distribution diagrams of CDs (B), AuNPs@CDs(water) (D) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (F). (Insets in A, C and E are HRTEM images). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS full scan spectra (A) and high-resolution C1s spectra (B) of CDs (a), separated oxidized CDs(water) (b) and oxidized CDs(ethanol) (c). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
XPS high-resolution Au4f spectra (A) and C1s spectra (B) of AuNPs@CDs(water) (a) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (b). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) FTIR spectra of AuNPs@CDs(water) (a) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (b); (B) Zeta potential of AuNPs@CDs synthesized in solvent with different contents of ethanol (0.0%–100.0%, v/v). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

UV–vis spectra (A) and linear relationship between A 690 nm and ethanol content (B) of AuNPs@CDs synthesized in solution with different ethanol contents (0.0%–100.0%, v/v) (Inset is the colorimetric image); UV–vis spectra (C) and linear relationship between A 690 nm and different spiked ethanol contents (0.0%, 2.5%, 5.0%, 10.0%, 15.0%, 20.0%, v/v) of AuNPs@CDs synthesized in Jiangxiaobai (D); and UV–vis spectra (E) and linear relationship between A 690 nm and different spiked ethanol contents (0.0%, 5.0%, 10.0%, 15.0%, 20.0%, 25.0%, v/v) of AuNPs@CDs synthesized in Erguotou (F). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Schematic diagram of solvent mediated in situ growth, assembly, and aggregation of AuNPs@CDs.

head.ce:title: Solvent-mediated in situ growth and assembly of gold nanoparticles@carbon dots for rapid colorimetric nonenzymatic alcohol sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha 410083, Hunan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Natural Product Research Laboratory, Guangxi Baise High-tech Development Zone, Baise, 533612, Guangxi, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Natural Product Research Laboratory, Guangxi Baise High-tech Development Zone, Baise 533612, Guangxi, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, Changsha, 410078, Hunan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, Changsha 410078, Hunan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fundamental mechanism underlying solvent-mediated carbon dots (CDs) reduction of HAuCl4 to form gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), and the surface characteristics deciding their dispersion and aggregation, are largely unclear. Monodispersed core-shell AuNPs@CDs(water) (21.9 ± 2.7 nm) were in situ synthesized in water, while aggregated AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) (102.6 ± 22.0 nm) were found in ethanol. We propose that the transition is mediated by solvent through changing the solubility and reducing performance of CDs, and interactions among oxidized CDs, AuNPs and solvent. Water with higher polarity promotes CDs to rapidly reduce Au3+ into Au0, and forms a stronger electrical double layer for slow growth of nearly neutral AuNPs. Concurrently, oxidized CDs(water) with higher contents of carboxyl groups coordinate with AuNPs, and stabilize AuNPs by strong electrostatic repulsive forces (zeta potential at −23.5 mV). Red color was obtained in water system. However, ethanol with lower polarity deduces the aggregation of AuNPs. Moreover, oxidized CDs(ethanol) with lower contents of carboxyl groups coordinate with AuNPs, and interact with each other through strong hydrogen bonds to form carboxyl dimers, which further deduces aggregation. Then blue color was achieved in ethanol system. Therefore, a facile AuNPs@CDs-based colorimetric ethanol sensor (linear range, 20.0%–90.0%, v/v, R 2 = 0.999) with high selectivity was proposed by measuring the plasmon resonance absorbance change of AuNPs. Real sample analysis of liquors based on the standard addition method showed satisfying accuracy and excellent reproducibility. The proposed method provides new insight into nonenzymatic ethanol detection, and valuable information for the development of AuNPs-based colorimetric sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of quasi-spherical carbon nanomaterials with ultrasmall particle size (<10 nm) and adjustable surface oxygen-containing groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl groups) [1]. CDs can be fabricated from varieties of nontoxic sources by simple and economical synthesis procedures with tunable structures, controllable photoluminescence, outstanding photostability, excellent biocompatibility, and environmental friendliness [2–6]. Thus, CDs have attracted much attention in sensing, bioimaging, and drug delivery [1,7,8]. In addition, the ultrasmall size, up-converted photoluminescence, as well as fast interfacial charge transfer and remarkable conductivity promote the rational design of CDs for efficient photocatalysis and photoelectrochemical applications [9,10]. Interestingly, CDs present strong electron donor behavior, and can be designed as novel reductants to promote the nucleation of noble metal nanoparticles (e.g., gold nanoparticles, AuNPs [11]; silver nanoparticles [12]; gold–silver nanoparticles [13]; silver–copper nanoparticles [14]). CDs can also stabilize metal nanoparticles from aggregation in pure water solution due to their excellent surface-active groups, and the formation of stable metal nanoparticles@CDs hybrids can facilitate the dispersion of CDs [15]. Generally, solvent composition and stabilizer functionalization strongly affect the growth, and assembly of metal nanoparticles [16,17]. Franco-Ulloa et al. developed theoretical models to disclose the complex interplay among the ionic strength of solvents, particle size of metal nanoparticles, surface charge density, and stability [16]. Hussain et al. found that the growth of AuNPs gradually increased with a decrease in the polarity index of the reaction solvent [18]. Grueso et al. reported that ethanol can mediate AuNPs aggregation and DNA structural conformation to modify AuNPs/DNA interactions [19]. However, almost all studies have focused on the facile preparation of stable metal nanoparticles@CDs in pure water solution and investigation of their applications [15], while the effect of solvent on the growth and interaction of metal nanoparticles@CDs is still not explored.

AuNPs (size between 10 and 100 nm) with tunable optical properties (e.g., high molar absorption coefficient, ca. 108–1010 M−1‧cm−1) have been widely applied in chemical and biological sensing, optical devices, drug delivery, and therapeutic agents [20,21]. AuNPs are usually synthesized from hydrogen tetrachloroaurate (HAuCl4) using citrate and sodium borohydride as reductants [22]. Changes in the solvent dielectric and binding ligand have a great influence on the size, shape, morphology, and interparticle distance of AuNPs, and their surface plasmonic properties [23,24]. DNA, amino, protein, thiol, toxic metal ions, bacterial species, pH value and polarity of solution have been found to easily mediate the morphologies and aggregation behavior of AuNPs, and the notable color change of AuNPs solution between ruby red and deep blue [25,26]. Obviously, target-driving reassembly of AuNPs for fabrication of colorimetric biosensors presents outstanding advantages, such as simple operation, rapid response, sensitive detection, and lack of expensive equipment, which has then drawn remarkable research interests [27]. In most cases, AuNPs were first synthesized, and then attention has largely focused on how the involvement of ligands or solvents changed the morphology or dispersion/aggregation state of AuNPs [24,28]. However, the effect of ligands or solvents on the in situ growth and assembly of AuNPs, especially for CDs in nonaqueous solvents, remains pending. From a fundamental standpoint, the solvent influences the solubility and reducing performance of CDs, modulates the interaction among AuNPs surface, CDs, and solvent molecules, and promise distinct growth and assembly behaviors in AuNPs@CDs formation process. Thus, it is challenging and important to investigate the role of solvent and CDs in AuNPs growth and assembly. Furthermore, knowledge of AuNPs@CDs formation mechanism in organic media (e.g., ethanol) will help to meet great challenges in practical colorimetric assays.

In this work, the in situ growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs in water‒ethanol solution were investigated (Scheme 1 ). CDs with abundant –OH groups were hydrothermally synthesized by citric acid and urea in dimethylformamide (DMF) [29]. By simple mixing CDs and HAuCl4, CDs acted as reductant to reduce HAuCl4 into AuNPs. In pure water solution, CDs with higher solubility and reducing performance to rapidly reduce Au3+ into Au0 monomers. Water with higher polarity and oxidized CDs(water) with more carboxyl groups mediate the growth of AuNPs and prevent them from aggregation. As a result, monodispersed core-shell AuNPs@CDs(water) are formed, and a red colored solution is achieved. By comparison, in ethanol solution, CDs with relatively lower solubility and reducing performance to reduce Au3+ into Au+ and then to Au0 monomers. After that, ethanol with lower polarity, oxidized CDs(ethanol) with fewer carboxyl groups, Au3+ and Au + residues establish specific crosslinked networks between AuNPs, and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) aggregates form. Here, the solution became blue colored. Thus, solvent can mediate the in situ growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs. Such phenomenon can be fabricated into a facile, fast, sensitive, and nonenzymatic method for the direct detection of ethanol content, and satisfactory results were obtained. In addition, the study could provide new insight into the surface of AuNPs@CDs, and reveal a fascinating side of the hybrid materials.
Details of the reagents and apparatus are described in the Electronic Supplementary Material.

CDs were synthesized from citric acid and urea in DMF solution by a one-pot solvothermal method according to our previous work [29]. Briefly, citric acid (1.0 g) and urea (1.5 g) were uniformly dispersed in DMF (10.0 mL), and then transferred to a Teflon-sealed autoclave reactor (80 mL). After being heated at 180 °C for 8 h, the reaction products were naturally cooled, and filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. After that, NaOH solution (0.2 g‧mL−1, 40 mL) was added, and the precipitate was washed with water three times and collected by centrifugation. Finally, CDs were obtained after freeze drying and stored in vacuum drier for further use.

CDs solution (200.0 μg‧mL−1) and HAuCl4 solution (10.0 mmol‧L−1) were separately prepared and stored in the dark at 4 °C. For synthesis of AuNPs@CDs, CDs (260.0 μL) and HAuCl4 (20.0 μL) were added to water or ethanol solution (1.0 mL), and then incubated at 37 °C for 15 min to synthesize AuNPs@CDs(water) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol). Moreover, solutions with different pH values (2–11) was prepared.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Separation of oxidized CDs from AuNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The obtained AuNPs@CDs(water) or AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) solution was centrifuged at 10000 rpm/min 3 times, each for 15 min. The supernatant was collected and dried under vacuum to obtain oxidized CDs(water) or CDs(ethanol), which were then stored in a vacuum drier for further use.

CDs (200.0 μg‧mL−1, 260.0 μL) and HAuCl4 (10.0 mmol‧L−1, 20.0 μL) were added into 1.0 mL of solvent containing 0.0%, 10.0%, 20.0%, 30.0%, 40.0%, 50.0%, 60.0%, 70%, 80%, 90.0%, and 100.0% (v/v) ethanol, respectively. Then the solutions were incubated at 37 °C for 15 min. UV–vis spectra were collected from 200 to 800 nm, and the intensity of the characteristic absorption peaks at 690 nm (A 690 nm) was recorded for quantitative analysis. For the assay of selectivity towards ethanol, proline, alanine, methylalanine, sarcosine, hydroxyproline, leucine, serine, homocysteine, Zn(NO3)2, CoCl2, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, and NaCl (20.0 μL, 50.0 mmol‧L−1) were added to the reaction system, and UV–vis spectra were recorded after 15 min incubation at 37 °C. For the assay of selectivity towards methanol and isopropanol, CDs (200.0 μg‧mL−1, 260.0 μL) and HAuCl4 (10.0 mmol‧L−1, 20.0 μL) were added into 1.0 mL of solvent containing 0.0%, 10.0%, 20.0%, 30.0%, 40.0%, 50.0%, 60.0%, 70%, 80%, 90.0%, and 100.0% (v/v) methanol or isopropanol, and the UV–vis spectra were recorded after 15 min incubation at 37 °C.

The standard addition method was chosen in the analysis of Jiangxiaobai liquor (Chongqing Jiangxiaobai Liquor Co., Ltd., Chongqing, China), and Erguotou liquor (Beijing Red Star (Stock) Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) to resist interference from the matrix. Briefly, 500 μL of liquor, 500 μL of ethanol solution (0.0%, 5.0%, 10.0%, 20.0%, 30.0%, 40.0%, 50%, v/v), CDs (200.0 μg‧mL−1, 260.0 μL) and HAuCl4 (10.0 mmol‧L−1, 20.0 μL) were mixed. A 690 nm values were measured after incubation at 37 °C for 15 min.

It has been reported that CDs prepared from citric acid and urea are rich in hydroxyl and amino groups [29]. In addition, the surface hydroxyl groups can be oxidized into carboxyl groups in the presence of HAuCl4; thus, CDs have been designed to regulate the growth of AuNPs [30].

As shown in Fig. 1 A (a) and B (a), the absorbance peak of CDs at about 270 nm corresponds to the π‒π* transition of the graphite sp2 domain, and another peak at about 532 nm indicates the n‒π* transition of functional groups containing heteroatoms. The FTIR spectrum reveals a broad stretching vibration peak of O–H/N–H (3660–3100 cm−1), a stretching vibration peak of CO (‒COOH) at 1719 cm−1, a stretching vibration peak of CO (‒CONH2‒) at 1630 cm−1, and stretching vibration peak of CC (1580 cm−1) (Fig. 2 A (a)). Then the morphology of the as-synthesized CDs was analyzed by TEM (Fig. 3 A). CDs disperse well with an average particle diameter of 2.1 ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 3B), and the lattice fringe at 0.21 nm (inset in Fig. 3A) corresponds to the (100) crystal surfaces of graphene [29]. In the XPS full scan spectrum (Fig. 4 A(a)), three major peaks at 283.8, 398.9, and 530.4 eV are ascribed to C1s, N1s, and O1s, and their contents are 62.2%, 11.3%, and 26.5%, respectively. The results showed that CDs were successfully synthesized with abundant hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface.

Solution has a significant effect on the growth and assembly of AuNPs [18]. Herein, CDs synthesized by citric acid and urea were selected as reductant, and we discussed the optical properties and morphology of AuNPs@CDs obtained by mixing CDs with HAuCl4 in water and ethanol system (i.e., AuNPs@CDs(water) and AuNPs@CDs(ethanol)).

Fig. 1 clearly shows that the purple color of CDs solution fades away after the addition of HAuCl4. In water solution, a characteristic red color for dispersed AuNPs occurs (inset in Fig. 1A). The UV–vis absorption peak for the n‒π* transition in CDs at 532 nm disappears, and oxidized CDs(water) present a n‒π* transition peak at 417 nm (Fig. 1A). At the same time, a new absorption peak at about 528 nm increases gradually with increasing reaction time to 15 min (Figs. 1A and S1), corresponding to the reported localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) of well-dispersed AuNPs [20]. XRD with intense and sharp peaks at the (111), (200), (220), (311) and (222) facets (JCPDS 04–0784) also shows the successful synthesis of AuNPs (Fig. 2B(a)). Moreover, the XPS full scan spectrum exhibits four major peaks at 284.8 (C1s), 399.9 (N1s), 531.7 (O1s), and 83.5 eV (Au4f) (Fig. S2a). TEM and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) were applied to reveal the morphology of synthesized AuNPs@CDs(water). Monodispersed spherical AuNPs@CDs(water) with a particle size of 21.9 ± 2.7 nm can be observed (Fig. 3C and D). HRTEM clearly shows a core-shell structure with lattice spaces of 0.235 nm and 0.210 nm (insets in Fig. 3C), corresponding to the (111) crystal surfaces of Au and (100) crystal surfaces of graphene [11,29]. Thus, CDs can reduce HAuCl4 into AuNPs to form stable core-shell AuNPs@CDs hybrids in water system.

Unlike in water system, the color becomes blue after CDs mixing with HAuCl4 in ethanol (inset in Fig. 1B). At this time, oxidized CDs(ethanol) exhibit n‒π* transition peaks at 317 and 417 nm (Fig. 1B). In addition, a broad redshift absorbance around 575–700 nm appears, which is ascribed to the typical LSPR signal of aggregated AuNPs [20]. XRD and XPS present the typical peaks for AuNPs (Fig. 2B(b), S2b), and the TEM image describes the aggregated spherical clusters of AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) with a particle size of 102.6 ± 22.0 nm (Fig. 3E‒F). Dynamic light scattering (DLS) results also show that with the increase of ethanol content from 0.0% to 100.0% (v/v), hydrodynamic diameter of AuNPs@CDs increased linearly from 26.8 to 374.0 nm (Fig. S3), implying the possibility of aggregation of AuNPs in ethanol. These results prove that solvent can mediate the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of solvent mediating the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs
ce:section.ce:para: It is known that solution can affect bond formation, reaction free energy, and activation energy, and then has a great influence on the conversion efficiency of oxidation/reductant products [31]. CDs have higher solubility in water than in ethanol, which might present better reducing performance in water. The fluorescence image of CDs is red, while it presents a green color after reaction with HAuCl4 (Fig. S4). Fluorescent spectra for oxidized CDs(water) and CDs(ethanol) were similar (Fig. S4). However, their UV–vis spectra present significant differences (Fig. 1), which might come from the different degrees of surface oxidation [32]. In the FTIR spectra, it is obvious that the relative intensity of the stretching vibration of the carboxy group at 1719 cm−1 increases with the order: CDs, oxidized CDs(ethanol), and oxidized CDs(water) (Fig. 2A). The results reveal that hydroxyl groups in CDs can be oxidized into carboxyl groups by HAuCl4, and more carboxyl groups are produced in water system. The relative atomic percentages of O in oxidized CDs(water) increased obviously (Fig. 4A). In the high-resolution C1s spectra (Fig. 4B), the relative CO content is 25.9% for oxidized CDs(water), 17.9% for oxidized CDs(ethanol), and 10.2% for CDs.

Both solvent and stabilizer play an important role in mediating the growth and assembly of AuNPs. Two stages can be included during AuNPs formation, i.e., solvent- and reductant-induced growth of Au0 monomers followed by stabilizer-caused assembly of AuNPs [18]. In water system, dispersed core-shell AuNPs@CDs are produced (Fig. 3C), and in the XPS Au4f spectra, only two characteristic peaks at 87.2 eV and 83.5 eV for 4f5/2 and 4f7/2 peak of Au0 are observed (Fig. 5 A(a)). CDs with higher solubility in water will transfer more electrons for fast nucleation of Au0 monomer. Water with higher polarity provides higher surface charge on AuNPs surface, and forms a stronger electrical double layer that leads to slow growth of nearly neutral AuNPs and prevents AuNPs from further growth [18,33]. After that, oxidized CDs(water) with more carboxyl groups covalently bind with the surface of AuNPs as stabilizer, which can be deduced from the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration peaks of carboxyl groups at 1530 and 1357 cm−1, and the stretching vibration peak of carboxyl monomer at 1745 cm−1 for AuNPs@CDs(water) (Fig. 6 A (a)) [34]. Moreover, the abundant surface carboxyl group of oxidized CDs(water) have strong H-bond interaction with water, resulting in high solubility of AuNPs@CDs(water) [35]. On the other hand, the charge of surface absorbed CDs(water) affects the zeta potential. The negative zeta potential of AuNPs@CDs(water) was shown to have the highest value (−23.5 mV) (Fig. 6B), indicating strong electrostatic repulsive forces between AuNPs@CDs(water). Thus, it can be proposed that higher solubility and stronger electrostatic repulsion elevate the dispersion of AuNPs@CDs(water) [35‒36].

Whereas in ethanol solution, aggregated AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) were observed (Fig. 3E). In the XPS Au4f spectra, peaks at 91.0, 90.5 and 88.0 eV are assigned as 4f5/2 peaks of Au3+, Au+, and Au0, while peaks at 87.3, 86.9, and 84.6 eV are the 4f7/2 peaks of Au3+, Au+, and Au0 (Fig. 5A(b)). The relative contents of Au3+, Au+, and Au0 are 59.0%, 22.4% and 18.6%, respectively. Obviously, CDs in ethanol present lower solubility and mild reducing power, which reduces Au3+ into Au+ and then into Au0 monomer to form AuNPs. At this time, ethanol with lower polarity forms a weaker electrical double layer around AuNPs, which deduces the aggregation or high growth rate [33]. The binding energies of O–CO (288.9 eV), CO (288.1 eV), and C–O (286.8 eV) for AuNPs@CDs synthesized in ethanol are higher than those (288.6, 287.9, and 286.6 eV) for AuNPs@CDs synthesized in water (Fig. 5B), confirming the stronger interaction of AuNPs@CDs in ethanol. It is proposed that reduced solubility of oxidized CDs in ethanol leads to the great decrease of solvent accessible to the surface of AuNPs@CDs(ethanol), and then favors the linkage between oxidized CDs(ethanol) and AuNPs, [35]. Hence, AuNPs@CDs in ethanol tend to aggregate. In addition, the strong H-bond interaction between oxidized CDs(ethanol) (characterized by carboxyl dimer peak at 1717 cm−1 in FTIR, Fig. 6A), and the established specific crosslinked networks between AuNPs, Au3+ and Au + residues contribute to the formation of aggregates through reducing the interparticle distance [36‒37]. Moreover, the gradually decreased zeta potential values of AuNPs@CDs with increasing ethanol contents were observed (Fig. 6B), indicating that decreased electrostatic repulsion also drives the aggregation of AuNPs@CDs in ethanol [35].

Thus, solvent mediates the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs by changing the solubility and reducing performance of CDs, and interactions among solvent, AuNPs and oxidized CDs. The sensitivity of AuNPs@CDs to solvent inspired us to fabricate an ethanol indicator based on colorimetric detection.

Ethanol is widely used in medical, pharmaceutical, and food industries, and rapid quantification of ethanol is important for the quality control of alcoholic beverages. Many techniques have been explored for ethanol sensing, such as chromatographic techniques, electrochemical methods and optical sensing systems [38,39]. Although they meet the merits of sensitivity and selectivity, there are also some challenges, including expensive and sophisticated instrumentation, high requirements for testing conditions (e.g., specific temperature and pH for enzyme-based sensors), and complicated operation procedures [40]. Therefore, it is requisite to fabricate a facile, rapid, cost-effective, and sensitive nonenzymatic method for ethanol quantification.

AuNPs-based colorimetric methods have triggered intensive research because of the rapidness and simplicity [20]. Firstly, the influence of CDs and HAuCl4 concentrations, reaction time, temperature and pH on the growth and stability of AuNPs were investigated. From the UV–vis spectra and the relationship between the concentration ratios of CDs and HAuCl4 and absorbance (Fig. S5A), it can be seen that absorbance for monodispersed AuNPs@CDs(water) increases with increasing concentration ratios. However, higher concentration of CDs results in the appearance of CDs aggregates, which may yield the aggregation of AuNPs@CDs [18]. Thus, absorption peak at about 690 nm for aggregated AuNPs@CDs(ethanol) appears when concentration ratios of CDs and HAuCl4 are larger than 300:1 (Fig. S5A). Consequently, a concentration ratio of CDs and HAuCl4 of 260:1 was the most suitable. The reaction between CDs and HAuCl4 completes within 15 min (Fig. S5B). Temperature may affect the growth, assembly, and stability of AuNPs@CDs by changing the viscosity of solvent and the surface potential of particles [41]. Here, reaction temperature can be set as 37 °C (Figs. S6A and C). Higher content of H+ disturbs the electron donating ability of CDs to Au3+, while excess of OH− can react with Au3+ to reduce its reactivity [42]. When pH is 3–10, the growth and stability of AuNPs@CDs had negligible change (Fig. S6B). Therefore, pH at 7.0 was chosen for further analysis.

To develop this method in practical applications, potential interferences (e.g., 1.0 × 103 μmol‧L−1 of amino acids and metal ions, equivalent to the amount in actual liquor samples [43]) are selected to evaluate the selectivity. As shown in Fig. S7A, negligible effects except ethanol can be observed, indicating the high selectivity of the developed method. What is more, AuNPs@CDs show high stability in high concentration (3.0 × 103 μmol‧L−1) of two major saline interferences (NaCl and KCl) (Fig. S7B). Under the optimum conditions, AuNPs@CDs synthesized in solution with different ethanol contents (0%–100.0%, v/v) were investigated. The absorption peak at 690 nm gradually increases with increasing ethanol content, and the solution displays an obvious color change from red to blue (Fig. 7 A). A good linear relationship between A 690 nm and ethanol content was fitted as A = 6.02 × 10−3 x ‒ 0.043 (R 2 = 0.999), where A referred to absorbance at 690 nm and x was the ethanol content (%, v/v) (Fig. 7B). The linear range was 20.0%–90.0%, and the limit of detection (LOD) calculated by 3.3σ/k (σ is the standard deviation of 11 blank samples, and k is the slop of the linear equation) was 11.6%. The linear range and LOD are sufficient to directly determine the ethanol content in liquors.

Ethanol analogues (i.e., methanol, isopropanol) were also selected for evaluation of the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs. As shown in Figs. S7C and E, the wide peak at 575–700 nm for aggregated LSPR signal of AuNPs is observed. And a good linear relationship content can be fitted within 0–60.0% for methanol (Fig. S7D) and 10.0%–60.0% for isopropanol (Fig. S7F). However, it was noted that π‒π* and n‒π* transition peaks for oxidized CDs are very different, i.e., 276, 317, and 417 nm in ethanol, 280, 322, and 425 nm in methanol, 279, 317, and 423 nm in isopropanol (Fig. 7A, S7C and E). Furthermore, the relative intensities of these peaks also have large difference. Results implied that solvent had great influence on the reducing performance of CDs [13], and then oxidized CDs with different surface functional groups were produced in different alcohol solutions. Then the growth and assembly behavior of AuNPs@CDs can be affected, which can be observed from their different absorption and linear ranges. As a result, a multidimensional colorimetric data array can be achieved to distinguish and detect ethanol, methanol, and isopropanol using chemometrics methods, which can be investigated in detail in our future studies.

To reduce matrix interference and improve the accuracy of quantitative analysis, a standard addition method was chosen for liquor sample analysis. The ethanol content of liquor was set as zero, and a linear relationship between A 690 nm and added ethanol content was obtained as A = 9.28 × 10−3 x + 0.189 (R 2 = 0.983) (Fig. 7C and D) for Jiangxiaobai. The ethanol content was 40.7° calculated through the extrapolation method. And for Erguotou, the linear relationship was obtained as A = 7.04 × 10−3 x + 0.196 (R 2 = 0.950) (Fig. 7E and F), and the calculated ethanol content was 55.7°. The RSD of intra- and inter-day variations were calculated as less than 0.9% and 2.7%, respectively, indicating the satisfactory precision of the proposed method. Finally, the accuracy of the results was verified with alcoholmeter method (Chinese Standard method, GB/T 10345–2007, 42.9° and 56.0° for Jiangxiaobai and Erguotou) (Table S1). The results with less than 5.1% deviation between the two methods confirm the accuracy and practicality of the colorimetric sensing strategy based on the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs in different solvents.

In summary, the present work investigated the in situ growth and assembly behavior of AuNPs@CDs in water and ethanol systems, which was then applied for facile, rapid, sensitive, selective, and accurate colorimetric sensing of ethanol. For the first time, the influence of solvent (i.e., water and ethanol) on the growth and assembly of AuNPs@CDs was revealed, which was realized by affecting the solubility and reducing performance of CDs, interaction among AuNPs, oxidized CDs and solvent. Water deduced the formation of monodispersed core-shell AuNPs@CDs, whereas ethanol mediated the aggregation of AuNPs@CDs. Thus, based on the change in the absorption spectra of AuNPs@CDs, a straightforward method to sense ethanol was developed. Compared with other ethanol detection methods (e.g., chromatographic techniques, electrochemical methods), AuNPs@CDs-based colorimetric assay is easy-operating, economical, and no use of unstable enzymes. The wide linear range, high selectivity, and excellent accuracy are sufficient and efficient for ethanol determination in liquors. This work provides a novel analytical method for ethanol detection. Moreover, knowledge about the mechanism by which solvent mediates the growth and assembly of AuNPs will provide new considerations for the design of novel colorimetric sensors.

Chaoying Tong: Design of methodology, Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, preparation. Xia Tong: Investigation, Validation. Yuanxin Cao: Data curation, Formal analysis. Guihan Cai: Data curation, Validation. Tongtao Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Qisheng Wei: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Shuyun Shi: Conceptualization, Resources, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Ying Guo: Supervision, Project administration, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Key Research and Development projects in Hunan Province, China (2021NK2024), Postgraduate Independent Exploration and Innovation Project of Central South University, China (2021zzts0068), Postgraduate Scientific Research Innovation Project of Hunan Province (CX20210169).
